REST Data Connections in Palantir Foundry
Adam Casey
Follow
5 min read
·
Jul 4, 2025

1

Part 2: Integrating Live Weather Data with Foundry Using Webhooks

How I connected Open-Meteo’s API using REST Data Connections, Webhooks, Object Types, and Action Types

In Part 1, I introduced a personal Foundry project that analyzes trail running effort and pairs each route with a coffee roast recommendation. The next feature I wanted was real-time weather forecasting, pulled directly from the Open-Meteo API.

In this post, I’ll explain how I:
- Created a REST Data Connection to Open-Meteo
- Set up an on-demand Webhook
- Built a weather response Object Type
- Triggered the integration using an Action Type
- Surfaced it all in a Workshop application

— -

What Is a Data Connection in Foundry?

Data Connections in Foundry are the main way to connect external systems (like APIs or databases) to a Foundry environment.

Two key concepts:
- Sources: External systems Foundry can interact with (e.g., REST APIs, Snowflake, S3, CRM tools)
- Syncs: Instructions on how and when to bring that data into Foundry

There are two types of Sources:
- Direct Connections (e.g., public APIs like Open-Meteo or Hugging Face)
- Agent-Based Sources (used when connecting to secure or on-prem systems via local agents)

In my case, Open-Meteo is publicly accessible and easy to use, so I went with a Direct REST Connection via a Webhook (instead of a scheduled sync) to fetch data dynamically when a button in Workshop is clicked.

— -

Step 1: Create a REST Data Connection to Open-Meteo

From the Data Connection UI, I created a new Source using a “Direct connection” and was able to pretty easily get everything configured by clicking through Palantir’s setup process.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

These were the base settings I used:
- Name: open-meteo-api
- Base URL: https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast
- Project: I saved this under my trail app’s /api folder

Add a Network Egress Policy:
Foundry is locked down by default for security so I had to explicitly add a policy allowing outbound requests to api.open-meteo.com. Again, setting this up was pretty straightforward after I selected Request new policy.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

— -

Step 2: Create a Webhook on the Source

Staying in the Data Connection UI, I created a webhook with the GETHTTP method, named it “GET Current Weather Webhook” and set the path to:

/v1/forecast?latitude={{latitude}}&longitude={{longitude}}&daily=temperature_2m_max,temperature_2m_min,precipitation_sum,windspeed_10m_max&timezone=auto

After it was all put together, things were looking good!

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The {{latitude}}and {{longitude}} tokens will be dynamically substituted when the webhook is called (e.g., when a user selects a trail in the app).

Get Adam Casey’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

For the response, I chose to manually configure the output by extracting specific keys like Temperature-Wind Speed, Time and Wind Direction . There’s ton more data to be extracted from this request but I felt that these were the most important.

— -

Step 3: Build a Weather Object Type

To represent the API response from Open-Meteo, I created an Object Type which is a schema definition of a real-world event or entity. It includes the forecast metadata we set up as output parameters on our webhook earlier.

Defining this as an Object Type allowed me to structure the API output in a way that fits naturally into Workshop, downstream pipelines, and AIP logic.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Step 4: Trigger the Webhook Using an Action Type

With the webhook and object schema in place, I created an Action Type that links the two. This action takes lat and long variables as input, uses them in our webhook, and writes the response into a GET Weather Response object.

After a lot of head-banging, I learned it’s important to write rules that automatically fill in the appropriate fields or else I’d have no data to display:

With everything configured, it was finally time to test this out!

Step 5: Surface Weather Data in a Workshop App

Inside the Workshop application, check out Part 1 if you need a refresh on how that looks, I wired the new action into the existing UI. When a user selects a trail, they can click a “Get Weather” button to trigger the action. The resulting forecast is then displayed in a widget at the bottom of the page.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Because the forecast is tied to the trail that’s currently selected through other widgets in the app, the input variables will update automatically and give me specific, real-time forecasting for the trail I’m thinking about running.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Lessons Learned

- Data Connections gives you persistent, centralized control over how you connect to external systems.
- Webhooks allow on-demand, API-triggered integrations, perfect for real-time or user-driven workflows.
- Object Types help structure API responses into clean, linkable, visual components.
- Action Types serve as the glue, letting you turn external logic into an interactive app feature.

There’s so much more you can do with Data Connections, like using them in Transforms as part of Pipeline Builder process to work with complex data but that’s for another day.

— -

What’s Next?

Now that I have weather forecasting integrated, I think it’d be pretty cool to add features like UI warnings for hot, windy, or rainy conditions. Or even enabling batch weather pulls for multiple trails at once so it’s easier to find one with the best outlook.

In the next post, I’ll walk through how I’m building a “Upload Your Own Trail” feature — where users can drop in a GPX file and instantly get a personalized pace + weather forecast.
